attributes.stem:
A previously well 28-year-old man presents with a 6-month history of varying constipation and diarrhoea, abdominal pain, bloating and unintentional weight loss. He also feels fatigued and has a family history of similar symptoms, managed with dietary restriction. The physical examination is unremarkable apart from some mild abdominal distension and tenderness. Various laboratory investigations are performed and include:

  • Haemoglobin 102 g/L (130-170)

  • MCV 69 (80-98)

  • Vitamin D 20 nmol/L (50-200)

  • Folic acid 2.5 ng/mL (>3.0)

Further investigations are also arranged and the patient is prescribed strict adherence to a gluten-free diet.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following antibody tests will be the most sensitive for this patient's illness?
attributes.explanation:
This scenario suggests that the patient is most likely suffering from coeliac disease. Whilst his signs and symptoms could be attributable to other illnesses, but paired with his family history of a similar illness and his physician prescribing him a strict gluten-free diet makes coeliac disease the most likely diagnosis. His physician would have first ordered investigations before making a diagnosis and that would most probably have included anti-tissue transglutaminase (tTG) IgA and an intestinal biopsy. Anti-tissue transglutaminase (tTG) IgA is an enzyme that modifies gluten proteins and enhances their immunogenicity. In coeliac disease, the body produces antibodies against tTG, and the most sensitive and specific test for these antibodies is the anti-tTG IgA test. The anti-tTG IgA test has a sensitivity of 95% and a specificity of 98%.

Anti-endomysial (EMA) IgG is an indirect immunofluorescence test that detects antibodies against the endomysial layer of the smooth muscle cells of the small intestine. This test has a high specificity (99%) but a lower sensitivity (85%) compared to the tTG IgA test. It is considered a confirmatory test if the anti-tTG IgA test is positive.

Anti-gliadin (AGA) IgA is an antibody that binds to gliadin, one of the components of gluten. This test is less specific and less sensitive than the tTG IgA test and is no longer recommended for the diagnosis of coeliac disease.

Anti-reticulin (ARA) IgA is an antibody that binds to the reticulin fibres of the smooth muscle cells of the small intestine. This test has low specificity and sensitivity and is no longer recommended for the diagnosis of coeliac disease.

Anti-Saccharomyces cerevisiae (ASCA) IgG is an antibody that is associated with Crohn's disease, not coeliac disease. This test has no diagnostic value in the diagnosis of coeliac disease.
learningPoints: Anti-tissue transglutaminase (tTG) IgA is the most sensitive antibody test for the diagnosis of coeliac disease.
references: Caio G, Volta U, Sapone A, Leffler DA, De Giorgio R, Catassi C, Fasano A. Celiac disease: a comprehensive current review. BMC Med. 2019 Jul 23;17(1):142. doi: 10.1186/s12916-019-1380-z. PMID: 31331324; PMCID: PMC6647104. Available from: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/31331324/

Ciacci C, Bai JC, Holmes G, Al-Toma A, Biagi F, Carroccio A, Ciccocioppo R, Di Sabatino A, Gingold-Belfer R, Jinga M, Makharia G, Niveloni S, Norman GL, Rostami K, Sanders DS, Smecuol E, Villanacci V, Vivas S, Zingone F; Bi.A.CeD study group. Serum anti-tissue transglutaminase IgA and prediction of duodenal villous atrophy in adults with suspected coeliac disease without IgA deficiency (Bi.A.CeD): a multicentre, prospective cohort study. Lancet Gastroenterol Hepatol. 2023 Nov;8(11):1005-1014. doi: 10.1016/S2468-1253(23)00205-4. Epub 2023 Sep 9. Erratum in: Lancet Gastroenterol Hepatol. 2024 Jan;9(1):e1. doi: 10.1016/S2468-1253(23)00397-7. PMID: 37696284. Available from: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/37696284/

Kaur N, Minz RW, Bhadada SK, Saikia B, Dayal D, Anand S, Joshi N, Singh J, Thapa BR, Kochhar RK, Vaiphei K. Role of anti-tissue transglutaminase IgA+IgG antibodies in detection of potential celiac disease in patients with type 1 diabetes. Indian J Med Res. 2019 Jan;149(1):18-25. doi: 10.4103/ijmr.IJMR_1136_16. PMID: 31115370; PMCID: PMC6507530. Available from: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6507530/
